headline: HOGS SURGE SIX PERCENT AS CATTLE CRASH
subheadline: Livestock split violently while grains drift on planting delays.
lead: Lean hogs exploded 6% to $104.50 while live cattle crashed 2.1% to $243.60, the biggest one-day split between the complexes since March. The fund flow tells the story: managed money is rotating out of cattle futures and into hogs on cutout strength, but cattle's breakdown says the seasonal support just cracked. Corn gained a half-cent to $4.77 on planting delays, but the real story is livestock tearing itself apart.
the_takeaway: Livestock split this hard means one complex is wrong about demand.
teaser: Hogs surge as cattle crash in historic one-day split.

one_number.unit: spread between hog and cattle percentage moves
one_number.context: The 8.1 percentage point gap between hogs (+6.0%) and cattle (-2.1%) is the widest single-day livestock split since the March processing disruptions. When protein complexes move this hard in opposite directions, it's either fund rotation or one sector got the demand story completely wrong. Today's cutout data suggests hogs called it right.

yesterdays_call.summary: Geopolitical premium hitting metals hard while ag markets stay domestic-focused.
yesterdays_call.note: Silver crashed 2.8% today, reversing yesterday's geopolitical bid entirely.

sections.title: Hogs Explode Six Percent
sections.body: Lean hogs surged 6% to $104.50, the biggest single-day gain since February cutout rallies. **Pork cutout strength** is driving fund money back into the complex after three weeks of sideways drift. Open interest jumped 8,000 contracts as managed money flipped from neutral to net long for the first time since April. The move breaks hogs out of the $98-$102 range that held for two weeks and puts $108 resistance back in play.
sections.catalyst: Pork cutout strength drives fund rotation into hog futures
sections.bottom_line: Fund money is back, cutout is supporting, $108 is the next test.

sections.title: Cattle Crash Through Support
sections.body: Live cattle crashed 2.1% to $243.60, falling well through the $245 floor that held since late April. **Feeder weakness** at $362.45 confirms the breakdown, and fund positioning shows net long liquidation for the third straight session. The box-beef cutout hasn't weakened materially, but the technical damage says seasonal support just cracked. Next real support sits at $240, then $235 if this gets ugly.
sections.bottom_line: Seasonal floor cracked, $240 support is the next line.

sections.title: Grains Drift Higher
sections.body: Corn gained a half-cent to $4.77 while soybeans eased a penny to $12.15, another range-bound session as **planting delays in Missouri and Iowa** keep weather premium alive. Corn's now 5 cents off the April low but still 10 cents below the March high, waiting for Tuesday's crop progress to show if the Belt is falling behind schedule. Wheat jumped 5 cents to $6.46 on continued spring drought concerns, but the move lacks fund conviction.
sections.catalyst: Rain delays in Missouri and Iowa compress planting window
sections.bottom_line: Planting delays keeping premium alive, but range-bound until data.

sections.title: Energy Costs Jump
sections.body: WTI crude surged 2.1% to $101.24 as **Iran peace talks collapsed** after Trump called Iran's response to U.S. proposals 'garbage.' The Strait of Hormuz crisis is back in play, crude inventories fell last week, and diesel margins are widening again. Natural gas eased to $2.91 but energy input costs are climbing just as planting season peaks. Fertilizer pressure continues as Mosaic scales back phosphate production on record sulfur costs.
sections.catalyst: Iran peace talks collapse reignites Strait of Hormuz premium
sections.bottom_line: Energy costs climbing at worst possible time for planting season.

outside_the_pit.title: House vote on year-round E15 expected this week
outside_the_pit.body: House Majority Leader Steve Scalise listed the bill to legalize nationwide year-round E15 sales for a vote this week. The ethanol lobby has pushed this for years, but timing with planting season and current energy costs gives it fresh urgency.

outside_the_pit.title: Cargill CEO joins Trump China trip amid soybean questions
outside_the_pit.body: Cargill CEO Brian Sikes is among 17 corporate executives traveling with President Trump to China this week. His presence raises possibilities for U.S. commodity purchase agreements, potentially shifting the soybean export calculus.

outside_the_pit.title: H5N1 spreads on California dairy farms via multiple sources
outside_the_pit.body: New study suggests H5N1 transmission on dairy farms isn't limited to direct milk contact, complicating containment efforts. The findings add another risk layer to an already stressed dairy sector dealing with margin pressure.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: The live/feeder ratio widened sharply today as lives crashed harder than feeders. When this ratio widens this fast, it's either telling you feedlot margins are about to compress or the cattle complex is pricing in a demand shock. The speed of today's move suggests the latter.

basis.headline: Corn basis firming in delayed planting areas
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis** is firming as elevators compete for old-crop bushels while farmers focus on planting. Missouri and Iowa locations are adding 5-8 cents over the past week as local supplies tighten. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern, but the delay premium is starting to show up in local markets.

weekly_thread.question: Will corn planting hit 45% by Friday to stay on the five-year average?
weekly_thread.status_text: Rain delays in Missouri and Iowa are putting pressure on the timeline. Tuesday's crop progress report will show if the Belt is falling meaningfully behind schedule.

the_more_you_know.title: The 8.1% livestock split: when protein complexes divorce
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 8.1 percentage point spread between hogs (+6.0%) and cattle (-2.1%) marks the widest single-day divergence since March processing disruptions. When protein complexes move this hard in opposite directions, it's either massive fund rotation or one sector completely misread demand fundamentals. History shows splits above 6 percentage points resolve within two weeks, usually when the lagging complex catches up to the leader. The cutout data suggests hogs called it right this time.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn below 35% planted adds weather premium
Weekly cattle slaughter: under 580K head confirms tight supply
Hog futures: hold $104 or yesterday's breakout was false
Iran-U.S. diplomatic response after peace talks collapse

daily_quote.text: The real key to making money in stocks is not to get scared out of them.

source_summary: CME futures, USDA reports, industry sources, weather data
